hook: You already know that triangles have angles summing to $180°$ and circles have a constant ratio $\pi$ — but contest geometry asks you to see *why* five seemingly random points are concyclic, or why three mysterious lines meet at a single point. Standard formulas won't get you there alone. Can you build the deeper intuition that turns a cluttered diagram into an elegant, inevitable proof?
concept: Every topic in this unit is about uncovering hidden structure — relationships between points, lines, and circles that aren't obvious until you apply the right lens, whether that's a classical theorem, a transformation, or a new coordinate system. The unifying idea is *invariance and configuration*: recognizing that certain quantities or relationships are preserved, which forces geometric objects to align in beautiful, provable ways.

topic_roadmap.description: You will master the concurrence theorems — centroid, circumcenter, incenter, orthocenter — and learn to deploy classical results like Ceva's and Menelaus's theorems to prove that lines and points align.
Develop the skill of identifying similar triangle pairs in complex figures and using proportional relationships to systematically compute unknown lengths in contest problems.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Power of a Point and Radical Axes
topic_roadmap.description: Apply the power of a point to relate products of lengths around circles, and use radical axes to prove concurrence and solve problems involving multiple intersecting or tangent circles.
Master inscribed angle theorems, Ptolemy's theorem, and the angle-chasing techniques needed to prove four points are concyclic and to extract length and angle information from cyclic configurations.

Apply the law of sines, law of cosines, and trigonometric identities as powerful computational tools to resolve lengths, angles, and areas in geometric configurations that resist purely synthetic approaches.
Learn to use the cross-ratio and projective transformations to collapse difficult collinearity and concurrence problems into elegant arguments that remain invariant under projection.

Master the technique of circle inversion to transform tangled configurations of circles and lines into simpler ones, turning seemingly intractable problems about tangency and intersection into straightforward computations.
